pillar.metaDescription: Find senior apartments near you: HUD, Section 202, Section 8 and 55+/62+ options explained. Get free help today — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
pillar.h1: Senior Apartments Near Me: How to Find Affordable Housing for Seniors
pillar.quickAnswer: Senior apartments near you typically include 55+ and 62+ communities, HUD Section 202 buildings, and Section 8 voucher rentals. Most subsidized options cap income at 50% of your area median income (AMI), and HUD reports waiting lists can run months to years, so apply to several at once.
pillar.intro: Finding the right senior apartment means understanding the difference between age-restricted communities, government-subsidized buildings, and rental-assistance programs. This guide breaks down every option in plain English.
pillar.heroSubhead: Compare 55+ communities, HUD Section 202, Section 8 vouchers, and affordable housing programs — and get free, friendly help finding what fits your budget.

sections.h2: What types of senior apartments are available near me?
sections.body: Senior apartments fall into a few main categories. Age-restricted market-rate communities (55+ or 62+) charge regular rent but limit residents by age. Government-subsidized buildings — like HUD Section 202 — set rent at roughly 30% of your income. Section 8 Housing Choice Vouchers help you rent privately while the program pays part of the rent. Low-Income Housing Tax Credit (LIHTC) properties offer reduced rents to qualifying seniors. Each has its own income rules and application process. Knowing which category fits your finances is the first step. For free help sorting through your options, call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427.

sections.h2: What is the difference between 55+ and 62+ apartments?
sections.body: In a 55+ community, federal housing law requires that at least 80% of units have one resident aged 55 or older. In a 62+ community, every resident must be at least 62 — these are often HUD-subsidized buildings like Section 202. The practical takeaway: 55+ communities are usually market-rate or tax-credit properties with more flexibility, while 62+ buildings are frequently the deeply subsidized, income-restricted options. If affordability is your priority, 62+ subsidized housing typically offers the lowest rents, though waiting lists tend to be longer.

sections.h2: How much do senior apartments cost?
sections.body: Cost depends entirely on the program. In HUD Section 202 and Section 8 housing, you generally pay about 30% of your adjusted monthly income toward rent — the government covers the rest. LIHTC tax-credit apartments charge a fixed reduced rent based on area median income, often more affordable than market rate but higher than Section 8. Market-rate 55+ communities charge standard local rents. Because subsidized rent is tied to your income, not a flat price, there's no single national figure. We can help you estimate what you'd pay — call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: Who qualifies for low-income senior housing?
sections.body: Most subsidized senior housing requires that your household income fall below a set percentage of your area median income (AMI). HUD Section 202, for example, requires very-low-income status — generally under 50% of AMI — with at least one household member aged 62 or older. Section 8 eligibility also centers on the 50% AMI threshold, with priority often given to seniors and people with disabilities. Limits vary widely by location and are updated each spring at huduser.gov. A licensed advisor at 1-800-MEDIGAP can walk you through whether you likely qualify.

sections.h2: How do I apply for senior apartments near me?
sections.body: Start by contacting your local Public Housing Authority (PHA) and searching the HUD Resource Locator at resources.hud.gov to find subsidized buildings nearby. For Section 202 properties, you apply directly to each building's management office. For Section 8 vouchers, you apply through your PHA when its waiting list is open. Because lists can be long and many close periodically, housing experts recommend applying to several programs and buildings at once. Keep copies of your income and ID documents ready. Need a hand getting started? Call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 for free guidance.

faqs.q: What are senior apartments?
faqs.a: Senior apartments are residential communities restricted by age — typically 55+ or 62+ — designed for older adults who can live independently. They range from market-rate communities to government-subsidized buildings like HUD Section 202, where rent is based on your income rather than a fixed price.

faqs.q: What is the minimum age for senior apartments?
faqs.a: Most senior apartments require residents to be 55 or 62, depending on the community. In 55+ housing, at least 80% of units must have a resident aged 55 or older. In 62+ housing — common among HUD-subsidized buildings — every resident must be at least 62.

faqs.q: How long is the wait for senior apartments?
faqs.a: Waiting times vary by location and program. HUD reports that subsidized housing waiting lists can run from several months to multiple years, and many Public Housing Authorities close their lists when demand is high. Applying to several buildings at once shortens your effective wait.

faqs.q: Do senior apartments include meals or care?
faqs.a: Standard senior apartments are for independent living and usually do not include meals or personal care. HUD Section 202 buildings often provide a service coordinator who connects residents to community supports like transportation. If you need daily care, assisted living or in-home care services are separate options.

faqs.q: Can I use a Section 8 voucher for a senior apartment?
faqs.a: Yes. A Section 8 Housing Choice Voucher lets you rent a private apartment — including many 55+ and 62+ communities — while the program pays part of your rent directly to the landlord. The unit must pass HUD's housing-quality inspection and the landlord must accept vouchers.

faqs.q: What income do I need to qualify for affordable senior housing?
faqs.a: Most subsidized senior housing requires income below 50% of your area median income (AMI), the very-low-income threshold HUD uses. Exact dollar limits depend on your county and household size and are published each spring at huduser.gov. Some programs also serve households up to 80% of AMI.

faqs.q: What is the difference between senior apartments and assisted living?
faqs.a: Senior apartments are for independent older adults and provide housing only — you handle your own cooking, cleaning, and care. Assisted living provides personal care, meals, and supervision for those who need daily help. Senior apartments cost far less because they don't include care services.

faqs.q: How can 1-800-MEDIGAP help me find senior housing?
faqs.a: 1-800-MEDIGAP is the trusted toll-free line for all things senior in America. Our team offers free, no-pressure help understanding HUD programs, Section 202, Section 8, and affordable senior apartment options near you. Call 1-800-633-4427 to get pointed in the right direction.

sections.h2: How do I find senior apartments near me?
sections.body: The fastest way to find senior apartments near you is the HUD Resource Locator at resources.hud.gov, which maps subsidized and age-restricted buildings by zip code. You can also contact your local Public Housing Authority (PHA) for a list of senior properties and voucher options. Market-rate 55+ communities are typically listed through local apartment search sites and property managers. Because subsidized buildings often have waiting lists, search broadly and apply to more than one. For free, personalized help narrowing your search, call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427.

sections.h2: What should I look for in a senior apartment?
sections.body: Beyond price and age requirements, consider accessibility features like grab bars, walk-in showers, and elevators; proximity to medical care, pharmacies, and transit; and whether the building offers a service coordinator. Subsidized buildings like Section 202 often include supportive services that help residents age in place. Ask about utility costs, parking, and pet policies up front. Visiting in person helps you gauge safety and community feel. Our team at 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you think through what matters most for your situation.

faqs.q: How do I find affordable senior apartments near me?
faqs.a: Use HUD's Resource Locator at resources.hud.gov and contact your local Public Housing Authority to find subsidized senior apartments by area. Apply to several buildings at once, since waiting lists are common. For free help, call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427.

faqs.q: What age do you have to be for senior apartments?
faqs.a: Most senior apartments require residents to be 55 or 62. In 55+ communities, at least 80% of units must have one resident aged 55 or older. In 62+ buildings — often HUD-subsidized — every resident must be at least 62.

faqs.q: Are senior apartments cheaper than regular apartments?
faqs.a: Subsidized senior apartments are usually much cheaper because rent is tied to your income — often about 30% of it — rather than market rate. Market-rate 55+ communities, however, charge standard local rents and may cost the same as regular apartments.

faqs.q: Do senior apartments have waiting lists?
faqs.a: Many subsidized senior apartments do. HUD reports waiting lists can run from months to years, and some Public Housing Authorities close their lists when demand is high. Applying to several buildings at once improves your odds of placement sooner.

faqs.q: Can 1-800-MEDIGAP help me find senior apartments near me?
faqs.a: Yes. 1-800-MEDIGAP, the trusted toll-free line for senior needs, offers free help understanding senior apartment options near you — including HUD, Section 202, and Section 8. Call 1-800-633-4427 for friendly, no-pressure guidance.

sections.h2: What types of senior housing are near me?
sections.body: Senior housing near you generally falls into independent living (55+/62+ apartments), subsidized housing (HUD Section 202 and public housing), and rental assistance (Section 8 vouchers). Independent living suits seniors who manage daily life on their own. Subsidized buildings set rent at roughly 30% of income for those who qualify financially. This page focuses on independent and affordable housing — not assisted living or nursing care, which provide hands-on medical support. To figure out which category fits you, call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 for free guidance.

sections.h2: How do I qualify for subsidized senior housing?
sections.body: Subsidized senior housing generally requires income below 50% of your area median income (AMI) — HUD's very-low-income threshold — with at least one household member meeting the age requirement, usually 62 for Section 202. Limits depend on your county and household size and update each spring at huduser.gov. You'll typically verify income, assets, and citizenship or eligible-immigration status. Some programs serve households up to 80% of AMI. A licensed advisor at 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you estimate whether you'd qualify before you apply.

faqs.q: What is the difference between senior housing and a nursing home?
faqs.a: Senior housing provides independent or affordable apartments for older adults who manage daily life on their own. A nursing home provides 24-hour skilled medical care for those who can't live independently. Senior housing costs far less because it doesn't include medical or personal care services.

faqs.q: How do I find senior housing near me?
faqs.a: Search HUD's Resource Locator at resources.hud.gov and contact your local Public Housing Authority to find subsidized and age-restricted senior housing by area. Apply to multiple buildings, since waiting lists are common. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 for free help.

faqs.q: Does Medicare pay for senior housing?
faqs.a: No. Medicare does not pay for rent or room and board in senior housing. It covers medical services, not housing. Affordable senior housing is funded through HUD programs like Section 202 and Section 8. 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you understand both your housing and Medicare options.

faqs.q: Who qualifies for senior housing?
faqs.a: Eligibility for subsidized senior housing usually requires meeting an age threshold (often 62) and having income below 50% of your area median income. Market-rate 55+ communities only require meeting the age limit. Exact income limits vary by county and are published at huduser.gov.

faqs.q: Can 1-800-MEDIGAP help me find senior housing?
faqs.a: Yes. 1-800-MEDIGAP is the trusted toll-free line for all senior needs in America. Our team offers free, no-pressure help understanding senior housing options near you. Call 1-800-633-4427 to get started.

sections.h2: Where do low income senior apartments come from?
sections.body: Low income senior apartments are funded through three main channels: HUD Section 202 (deeply subsidized buildings for very-low-income seniors 62+), public housing run by local authorities, and Low-Income Housing Tax Credit (LIHTC) properties, which offer reduced rents to qualifying older adults. Section 202 and public housing set rent at roughly 30% of your income; LIHTC apartments charge a fixed reduced rent based on area median income. Each has separate applications. To find which programs operate near you, search resources.hud.gov or call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427.

sections.h2: How do I qualify for low income senior apartments?
sections.body: Qualifying generally means having income below 50% of your area median income (AMI) — the very-low-income line HUD uses — and meeting the building's age requirement, often 62. LIHTC properties may serve households up to 60% of AMI. Exact dollar limits depend on your county and household size and are updated each spring at huduser.gov. You'll verify income, assets, and identity during the application. Because every program defines limits a little differently, a free call to 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you confirm where you're likely to qualify.

faqs.q: What counts as low income for senior apartments?
faqs.a: For most subsidized senior apartments, low income means earning below 50% of your area median income (AMI), HUD's very-low-income threshold. Some tax-credit properties serve up to 60% of AMI. Exact dollar amounts vary by county and household size and are published at huduser.gov.

faqs.q: How much is rent in low income senior apartments?
faqs.a: In HUD Section 202 and public housing, rent is generally about 30% of your adjusted monthly income, so it scales with what you earn. LIHTC tax-credit apartments charge a fixed reduced rent based on area median income, often higher than HUD housing but below market rate.

faqs.q: How do I find low income senior apartments near me?
faqs.a: Use HUD's Resource Locator at resources.hud.gov and contact your local Public Housing Authority to find low income senior apartments by area. Apply to several at once because waiting lists are common. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 for free help.

faqs.q: Is there a waiting list for low income senior apartments?
faqs.a: Usually yes. HUD reports waiting lists can run from months to years, and many properties close their lists when demand is high. Applying to multiple buildings at once is the best way to be housed sooner.

faqs.q: Can 1-800-MEDIGAP help me find low income senior apartments?
faqs.a: Yes. 1-800-MEDIGAP, the trusted toll-free line for senior needs, offers free help understanding low income senior apartment programs like Section 202 and LIHTC near you. Call 1-800-633-4427 for friendly guidance.

page.intro: HUD funds several programs that make housing affordable for seniors. Here's how each one works and how to qualify and apply.
page.heroSubhead: Understand the HUD programs behind affordable senior housing — Section 202, public housing, and Section 8 — and how to get in.

sections.h2: What HUD programs serve seniors?
sections.body: HUD supports senior housing through three main programs. Section 202 Supportive Housing for the Elderly funds buildings reserved for very-low-income adults 62 and older, often with a service coordinator who links residents to community supports. Public housing, run by local authorities, offers subsidized apartments to low-income households including seniors. The Section 8 Housing Choice Voucher lets seniors rent privately while HUD pays part of the rent. All three base your rent on income rather than a fixed price. To learn which fits you, call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427.

sections.h2: How does HUD calculate my rent?
sections.body: In HUD-subsidized senior housing — including Section 202, public housing, and Section 8 — you generally pay about 30% of your adjusted monthly income toward rent and utilities. HUD's subsidy covers the remaining cost up to a payment standard. Because rent scales with income, residents on Social Security or a small pension pay proportionally less. There's no single national rent figure. Your local Public Housing Authority calculates your exact share during the application. A free call to 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you understand what to expect before you apply.

sections.h2: How do I apply for HUD senior housing?
sections.body: For Section 202, apply directly to each building's management office. For public housing and Section 8 vouchers, apply through your local Public Housing Authority when its waiting list is open. Use the HUD Resource Locator at resources.hud.gov to find buildings and authorities near you. Have income documents, ID, and Social Security information ready. Because lists can be long and close periodically, apply to several programs at once. Need help getting organized? Call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 for free, step-by-step guidance.

faqs.q: What is HUD senior housing?
faqs.a: HUD senior housing refers to federally funded affordable housing programs serving older adults, including Section 202 Supportive Housing for the Elderly, public housing, and Section 8 vouchers. In these programs, rent is generally based on about 30% of your income rather than a fixed market price.

faqs.q: Who qualifies for HUD senior housing?
faqs.a: Most HUD senior housing requires income below 50% of your area median income (AMI) and meeting an age threshold, often 62 for Section 202. Exact income limits depend on your county and household size and are published each spring at huduser.gov.

faqs.q: How much does HUD senior housing cost?
faqs.a: In HUD-subsidized senior housing, you generally pay about 30% of your adjusted monthly income toward rent and utilities, with HUD covering the rest. Because it's tied to income, there's no single national price — residents with lower incomes pay proportionally less.

faqs.q: How do I apply for HUD senior housing?
faqs.a: Apply directly to Section 202 buildings through their management offices, and apply for public housing or Section 8 vouchers through your local Public Housing Authority. Find both at resources.hud.gov. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 for free help applying.

faqs.q: Does HUD senior housing include care services?
faqs.a: HUD housing provides apartments, not medical or personal care. However, Section 202 buildings often include a service coordinator who connects residents to community supports like transportation and meals. For hands-on daily care, assisted living is a separate option.

faqs.q: Can 1-800-MEDIGAP help with HUD senior housing?
faqs.a: Yes. 1-800-MEDIGAP is the trusted toll-free line for senior needs. Our team offers free help understanding HUD programs like Section 202 and Section 8 and finding buildings near you. Call 1-800-633-4427 for no-pressure guidance.

sections.h2: What is subsidized senior housing?
sections.body: Subsidized senior housing is apartment housing where a government program covers part of your rent, so you pay only a share based on income — generally about 30% in HUD programs. The main forms are HUD Section 202 (buildings reserved for very-low-income adults 62+), public housing, and Section 8 vouchers that subsidize private rentals. This differs from market-rate 55+ communities, which charge full local rent. Subsidies make independent living affordable for seniors on Social Security or a fixed income. To find subsidized buildings near you, call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427.

sections.body: Start with the HUD Resource Locator at resources.hud.gov, which maps subsidized buildings by zip code, and contact your local Public Housing Authority for Section 8 and public housing lists. For Section 202 buildings, apply directly to each property's management office. Because waiting lists are common and sometimes close, apply to several buildings at once. Keep income and identity documents ready to speed up the process. For free, friendly help building your shortlist, call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427.

faqs.q: What does subsidized senior housing mean?
faqs.a: Subsidized senior housing means a government program pays part of your rent so you only pay a share based on income — generally about 30% in HUD programs. It includes Section 202, public housing, and Section 8 vouchers, all designed to make housing affordable for low-income seniors.

faqs.q: How do I qualify for subsidized senior housing?
faqs.a: You generally need income below 50% of your area median income (AMI) and to meet the building's age requirement, often 62. Income limits vary by county and household size and are published each spring at huduser.gov. You'll verify income, assets, and identity when applying.

faqs.q: How do I find subsidized senior housing near me?
faqs.a: Use HUD's Resource Locator at resources.hud.gov and contact your local Public Housing Authority to find subsidized senior housing by area. Apply to several buildings at once since waiting lists are common. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 for free help.

faqs.q: Is there a wait for subsidized senior housing?
faqs.a: Often yes. HUD reports subsidized housing waiting lists can run from months to years, and many Public Housing Authorities close their lists when demand exceeds supply. Applying to multiple buildings improves your chance of being housed sooner.

faqs.q: Can 1-800-MEDIGAP help me find subsidized senior housing?
faqs.a: Yes. 1-800-MEDIGAP, the trusted toll-free line for senior needs, offers free help understanding subsidized housing programs like Section 202 and Section 8 near you. Call 1-800-633-4427 for no-pressure guidance.

faqs.q: Who qualifies for affordable senior housing?

faqs.q: How do I find affordable senior housing near me?

faqs.q: What is the cheapest type of senior housing?

page.intro: Section 8 is the nation's largest rental-assistance program, and many seniors use it to afford private apartments. Here's how it works.
page.heroSubhead: Learn how Section 8 vouchers help seniors rent privately at an affordable, income-based cost.

sections.h2: How does Section 8 work for seniors?
sections.body: The Section 8 Housing Choice Voucher lets you rent a private apartment — including many 55+ and 62+ communities — while the program pays part of your rent directly to the landlord. You generally pay about 30% of your adjusted income, and the voucher covers the rest up to a local payment standard. The unit must pass HUD's housing-quality inspection, and the landlord must accept vouchers. Some Public Housing Authorities offer special-purpose vouchers that prioritize seniors and people with disabilities. To learn whether Section 8 fits you, call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427.

sections.h2: How do I apply for a Section 8 senior voucher?
sections.body: You apply for Section 8 through your local Public Housing Authority (PHA) when its waiting list is open. Find your PHA using the HUD Resource Locator at resources.hud.gov. Because demand is high, many lists are years long and some close until a brief reopening, so check several nearby authorities. Have income documents, ID, and Social Security information ready. Once you receive a voucher, you have a set window to find a qualifying unit. For free help navigating the process, call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427.

faqs.q: What is Section 8 senior housing?
faqs.a: Section 8 senior housing uses the Housing Choice Voucher to help low-income seniors afford private rentals. The program pays part of your rent directly to the landlord while you pay about 30% of your income. The unit must pass a HUD quality inspection and the landlord must accept vouchers.

faqs.q: Do seniors get priority for Section 8?
faqs.a: Sometimes. Some Public Housing Authorities offer special-purpose vouchers or preferences that prioritize seniors aged 62+ and people with disabilities. Priorities vary by location, so check with your local authority. Waiting lists remain common even with a preference.

faqs.q: What are the income limits for Section 8 senior housing?
faqs.a: Section 8 eligibility generally requires income below 50% of your area median income (AMI), HUD's very-low-income threshold. There's no single national dollar figure — limits vary by county and household size and are updated each spring at huduser.gov.

faqs.q: How do I apply for Section 8 as a senior?
faqs.a: Apply through your local Public Housing Authority when its waiting list is open; find it at resources.hud.gov. Because lists can be long or closed, check several nearby authorities. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 for free help applying.

faqs.q: Can I use a Section 8 voucher in a senior apartment?
faqs.a: Yes. A Section 8 voucher can be used in many 55+ and 62+ communities as long as the unit passes HUD's housing-quality inspection and the landlord accepts vouchers. This lets you combine age-restricted living with rental assistance.

faqs.q: Can 1-800-MEDIGAP help with Section 8 senior housing?
faqs.a: Yes. 1-800-MEDIGAP, the trusted toll-free line for senior needs, offers free help understanding Section 8 vouchers and finding your local Public Housing Authority. Call 1-800-633-4427 for no-pressure guidance.

page.metaDescription: Section 202 senior housing for very-low-income adults 62+ — eligibility, rent, how to apply. Free help — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.quickAnswer: Section 202 Supportive Housing for the Elderly is a HUD program providing subsidized apartments for very-low-income adults 62 and older. At least one household member must be 62+, income must fall below 50% of area median income (AMI), and rent is generally about 30% of your income. Apply directly to each building's management office.
page.intro: Section 202 is HUD's flagship program built specifically for low-income seniors. Here's exactly how it works and how to apply.
page.heroSubhead: Understand HUD's Section 202 program — subsidized, supportive apartments designed for very-low-income seniors 62 and older.

sections.h2: What is Section 202 senior housing?
sections.body: Section 202 Supportive Housing for the Elderly is a HUD program that funds the construction and operation of apartment buildings reserved for very-low-income older adults. Properties receive a capital advance plus rental assistance, allowing residents to pay rent based on income — generally about 30%. A defining feature is the service coordinator, who links residents to community supports like cleaning, cooking, and transportation so they can age in place independently. Owners can also use a small per-unit amount each month toward those supportive services. To find Section 202 buildings near you, call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427.

sections.h2: Who qualifies for Section 202?
sections.body: To qualify for Section 202, at least one member of your household must be 62 or older, and your household income must fall below 50% of the area median income (AMI) where the property is located. Priority often goes to those who demonstrate the greatest need — showing you have trouble affording standard housing and benefit from supportive services. Income limits vary by county and household size and update each spring at huduser.gov. You'll verify income, assets, and identity during the application. A free call to 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you confirm whether you likely qualify.

sections.h2: How do I apply for Section 202 housing?
sections.body: Unlike Section 8 vouchers, you apply for Section 202 directly to each building's management office rather than through a central authority. Use the HUD Resource Locator at resources.hud.gov to find Section 202 properties near you, then contact each one to join its waiting list. Because demand is high, apply to several buildings at once and keep income and ID documents ready. Waiting times can run from months to years. For free, step-by-step help finding and applying to Section 202 buildings, call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427.

faqs.q: What is Section 202 senior housing?
faqs.a: Section 202 Supportive Housing for the Elderly is a HUD program that funds apartment buildings for very-low-income adults 62 and older. Residents pay rent based on income — generally about 30% — and buildings often include a service coordinator who connects them to supportive community services.

faqs.q: What is the age requirement for Section 202?
faqs.a: At least one member of your household must be 62 years of age or older to qualify for Section 202 housing. This makes it a 62+ program, distinguishing it from 55+ communities that allow younger residents under federal housing rules.

faqs.q: What are the income limits for Section 202?
faqs.a: Section 202 requires very-low-income status — generally income below 50% of the area median income (AMI) where the property is located. Exact dollar limits depend on your county and household size and are published each spring at huduser.gov.

faqs.q: Does Section 202 housing provide care?
faqs.a: Section 202 provides apartments, not medical care, but buildings typically include a service coordinator who links residents to community supports like cleaning, cooking, and transportation. This helps seniors live independently. For hands-on personal care, assisted living is a separate option.

faqs.q: How do I apply for Section 202 housing?
faqs.a: Apply directly to each Section 202 building's management office, not through a central authority. Find properties at resources.hud.gov and join several waiting lists at once. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 for free help finding and applying.

page.metaDescription: Find 62 and older apartments near you, including HUD Section 202 and subsidized buildings. Free help applying — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.quickAnswer: 62 and older apartments near you require every resident to be at least 62 and include many HUD Section 202 and subsidized buildings. In HUD-subsidized 62+ housing, rent is generally about 30% of income, and you typically need income below 50% of your area median income (AMI). Find them at resources.hud.gov.
page.intro: Apartments restricted to ages 62 and older include some of the most affordable HUD-subsidized housing. Here's how to find and qualify for them near you.
page.heroSubhead: Find 62+ apartments near you — including deeply subsidized HUD Section 202 buildings designed for older seniors.

sections.h2: What are 62 and older apartments?
sections.body: In a 62 and older community, every resident must be at least 62 years old — a stricter standard than 55+ communities, where only 80% of units need a resident 55 or older. Many 62+ buildings are HUD-subsidized, including Section 202 Supportive Housing for the Elderly, where rent is tied to income. This makes 62+ apartments a strong choice for seniors seeking the deepest affordability. Some 62+ buildings are market-rate as well. To find 62 and older apartments near you, call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 for free help.

sections.h2: How do I find and qualify for 62+ apartments near me?
sections.body: Search the HUD Resource Locator at resources.hud.gov and contact your local Public Housing Authority to find 62+ buildings, including Section 202 properties. For subsidized 62+ housing, you'll generally need income below 50% of your area median income (AMI); market-rate 62+ communities only require meeting the age limit. Income limits vary by county and household size and update each spring at huduser.gov. Apply to several buildings at once because waiting lists are common. For free guidance, call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427.

faqs.q: What does 62 and older apartments mean?
faqs.a: 62 and older apartments are communities where every resident must be at least 62 years old. This is stricter than 55+ housing, which only requires 80% of units to have a resident 55 or older. Many 62+ buildings are HUD-subsidized, like Section 202.

faqs.q: Are 62+ apartments cheaper than 55+ apartments?
faqs.a: Often, yes. Many 62+ buildings are HUD-subsidized, with rent set at about 30% of income, while 55+ communities are more often market-rate or tax-credit properties. The deepest discounts usually come from subsidized 62+ housing like Section 202.

faqs.q: Can a younger spouse live in a 62+ apartment?
faqs.a: Policies vary by building. In strict 62+ housing, all household members generally must be 62 or older, though some HUD programs allow a spouse or live-in caregiver. Check each building's specific rules. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 to help sort out the details.

faqs.q: How do I find 62 and older apartments near me?
faqs.a: Use HUD's Resource Locator at resources.hud.gov and contact your local Public Housing Authority to find 62+ apartments, including Section 202 buildings. Apply to several at once since waiting lists are common. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 for free help.

faqs.q: Can 1-800-MEDIGAP help me find 62+ apartments?
faqs.a: Yes. 1-800-MEDIGAP, the trusted toll-free line for senior needs, offers free help finding 62 and older apartments — including HUD Section 202 — near you. Call 1-800-633-4427 for no-pressure guidance.

children.keyword: how to apply for low income senior housing

page.metaTitle: How to Apply for Low Income Senior Housing | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: Step-by-step guide to applying for low income senior housing through HUD, Section 202 and Section 8. Free help — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.h1: How to Apply for Low Income Senior Housing
page.quickAnswer: To apply for low income senior housing, find buildings at resources.hud.gov, then apply directly to Section 202 properties and through your local Public Housing Authority for public housing and Section 8 vouchers. You'll verify income below 50% of area median income (AMI), ID, and age. Apply to several at once, since waiting lists are common.
page.intro: Applying for low income senior housing takes a few clear steps across multiple programs. Here's exactly how to do it and what to prepare.
page.heroSubhead: A clear, step-by-step guide to applying for HUD, Section 202, and Section 8 senior housing — and getting on the right waiting lists.

sections.h2: What are the steps to apply for low income senior housing?
sections.body: First, find buildings and programs near you using the HUD Resource Locator at resources.hud.gov and your local Public Housing Authority (PHA). Second, identify which programs you qualify for — Section 202 (62+), public housing, or Section 8 vouchers. Third, apply directly to each Section 202 building's management office, and apply through your PHA for public housing and vouchers when lists are open. Fourth, join several waiting lists at once. Fifth, respond promptly when contacted. For free, step-by-step help through all of this, call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427.

sections.h2: What documents do I need to apply?
sections.body: Most low income senior housing applications ask for proof of income (Social Security award letters, pension or pay statements, tax returns), government-issued photo ID, Social Security numbers for all household members, and proof of age. You may also need to document assets and citizenship or eligible-immigration status. Having these ready before you apply prevents delays and missed deadlines, which matter because some waiting lists close quickly. If you're unsure what a building requires, a free call to 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 can help you prepare your paperwork.

sections.h2: How long does it take to get approved?
sections.body: Timing varies widely. After you apply, your income and eligibility are verified, which can take weeks. The bigger factor is the waiting list — HUD reports lists can run from several months to multiple years, and many PHAs close their lists when demand is high. Applying to several buildings and programs at once is the single best way to be housed sooner. Stay reachable and update your contact information so you don't miss an offer. For help tracking your applications, call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427.

faqs.q: How do I apply for low income senior housing?
faqs.a: Find buildings at resources.hud.gov, then apply directly to Section 202 properties and through your local Public Housing Authority for public housing and Section 8 vouchers. Verify income, age, and ID, and apply to several lists at once. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP at 1-800-633-4427 for free help.

faqs.q: What documents do I need to apply for senior housing?
faqs.a: You'll typically need proof of income (Social Security or pension statements, tax returns), photo ID, Social Security numbers for household members, and proof of age. Some programs also require asset documentation and proof of citizenship or eligible-immigration status.

faqs.q: Can I apply for more than one senior housing program?
faqs.a: Yes, and you should. Applying to several buildings and programs at once — Section 202, public housing, and Section 8 — is the best way to be housed sooner, since waiting lists are often long and some close periodically. There's no penalty for joining multiple lists.

faqs.q: How long is the wait to get approved?
faqs.a: Eligibility verification can take weeks, but the waiting list is the bigger factor. HUD reports lists can run from months to years, and many authorities close their lists when demand is high. Applying to multiple programs at once shortens your effective wait.

faqs.q: Can 1-800-MEDIGAP help me apply for senior housing?
faqs.a: Yes. 1-800-MEDIGAP is the trusted toll-free line for senior needs. Our team offers free, step-by-step help finding programs, preparing documents, and getting on the right waiting lists. Call 1-800-633-4427 for friendly, no-pressure guidance.
